Switch the VM CPU type to `host` or pick a runtime that exposes AVX2. Verify inside the guest with `grep -m1 -oE 'avx[0-9a-z]*' /proc/cpuinfo` .\\nFull prerequisites and provider login flows live in the [OpenCode setup walkthrough](https://computingforgeeks.com/setup-opencode-ai-coding-agent/) .\\n\\n## Top-level command map\\n\\n|Command |Purpose |\\n| --- | --- |\\n|`opencode [project]` |Launch the TUI in the given directory (default). |\\n|`opencode run [message..]` |One-shot headless invocation. |\\n|`opencode serve` |Headless HTTP API server other clients can attach to. |\\n|`opencode web` |Headless server plus the OpenCode web UI. |\\n|`opencode attach \x3Curl>` |Attach a TUI to a running server. |\\n|`opencode acp` |Agent Client Protocol over stdin/stdout (IDE bridge). |\\n|`opencode auth` (alias `providers` ) |Manage providers and credentials. |\\n|`opencode models [provider]` |List models, optionally filtered. |\\n|`opencode mcp` |Manage MCP servers (add, list, auth, debug, logout). |\\n|`opencode agent` |Manage agents (list, create). |\\n|`opencode session` |List or delete sessions. |\\n|`opencode export [sessionID]` |Export session data as JSON. |\"\n      ]\n    },\n    {\n      \"url\": \"https://www.candede.com/articles/opencode-ai-coding-agents\",\n      \"title\": \"The Complete Guide to OpenCode: Architecting Your AI Coding...\",\n      \"publish_date\": \"2026-03-20\",\n      \"excerpts\": [\n        \"Tools come and go in the AI development space, but community momentum is the ultimate indicator of what will stick. If you are deciding where to invest your time, look at the ecosystem: while Claude Code sits at a respectable 67,000 GitHub stars, OpenCode has surged to over 105,000 stars芒聙聰grabbing 36,517 in a single month alone.\\n\\nThis velocity means better documentation, more community configurations, and a rapidly expanding plugin ecosystem. But beyond the hype, OpenCode represents a fundamental shift. It is essentially the open-source, MIT-licensed equivalent of Claude Code, but with a massive advantage: it is entirely provider-agnostic. When new models drop芒聙聰like OpenAI芒聙聶s GPT-5.3 codex outperforming previous benchmarks芒聙聰you aren芒聙聶t locked into a single ecosystem. You can seamlessly switch between Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, or even local models.\\n\\nHowever, if you are only using the vanilla agents that ship inside of OpenCode, your results will remain vanilla.\\nOpenCode is incredibly useful out of the box, but taking things to the next level requires you to stop treating it like a simple chat interface and start treating it like infrastructure. This means implementing custom agents to package your workflows, tools, and constraints so they compound and improve over time.\\n\\nHere is the ultimate, updated guide on how to conceptually understand, install, and master OpenCode, including everything you need to know to build specialized custom agents.\\n\\n## The 芒聙聹Three Layers芒聙聺 Mental Model\\n\\nDiagram illustrating the Agents, Rules, and Extensions layers in a flat, friendly blue and green design\\n\\nTo use OpenCode effectively, you need to understand its architecture. Think of the tool in three distinct layers:\\n\\n1. **The Agents Layer (Who is driving?)** : This dictates the AI teammate you are interacting with. Agents can be primary (driving the conversation) or sub-agents (handling focused tasks).\\n2. **The Rules Layer (How should they behave?\\n)** : Managed via the `agents.md` file, this layer acts as the onboarding document for your AI, defining project conventions, directory structures, and strict dos/don芒聙聶ts.\\n3. **The Extensions Layer (How do we automate?)** : This consists of Skills (reusable knowledge playbooks) and Commands (one-shot executable workflow shortcuts).\\n\\n## The Optimal Setup: WSL + VS Code\\n\\nIllustration of a monitor showing a dark-themed VS Code code editor on the left and a WSL terminal on the right\\n\\nWhile you can run OpenCode in various environments, the recommended setup for the best performance and terminal compatibility is running it on Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) paired with VS Code.\\n\\n**The Workflow Architecture:**\\n\\n* **VS Code** acts as your visual code editor.\\n* **WSL** acts as your runtime environment.\\n* **OpenCode** runs purely inside the WSL terminal, editing the exact same file system that VS Code has open.\"\n      ]\n    },\n    {\n      \"url\": \"https://opencode.ai/docs/commands/\",\n      \"title\": Commands\\n\\nCreate custom commands for repetitive tasks.\\n\\nCustom commands let you specify a prompt you want to run when that command is executed in the TUI.\\n\\n```\\n/my-command\\n```\\n\\nCustom commands are in addition to the built-in commands like `/init` , `/undo` , `/redo` , `/share` , `/help` . Learn more .\\n\\n* * *\\n\\n## Create command files\\n\\nCreate markdown files in the `commands/` directory to define custom commands.\\n\\nCreate `.opencode/commands/test.md` :\\n\\n.opencode/commands/test.md\\n\\n```\\n--- description : Run tests with coverage agent : build model : anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 --- Run the full test suite with coverage report and show any failures. Focus on the failing tests and suggest fixes.\\n```\\n\\nThe frontmatter defines command properties. The content becomes the template.\\n\\nUse the command by typing `/` followed by the command name.\\n\\n```\\n\\\"/test\\\"\\n```\\n\\n* * *\\n\\n## Configure\\n\\nYou can add custom commands through the OpenCode config or by creating markdown files in the `commands/` directory.\\n\\n* * *\\n\\n### JSON\\n\\nUse the `command` option in your OpenCode config :\\n\\nopencode.jsonc\\n\\n```\\n{ \\\"$schema\\\" : \\\"https://opencode.ai/config.json\\\" , \\\"command\\\" : { // This becomes the name of the command \\\"test\\\" : { // This is the prompt that will be sent to the LLM \\\"template\\\" : \\\"Run the full test suite with coverage report and show any failures. \\\\n Focus on the failing tests and suggest fixes.\\n\\\" , // This is shown as the description in the TUI \\\"description\\\" : \\\"Run tests with coverage\\\" , \\\"agent\\\" : \\\"build\\\" , \\\"model\\\" : \\\"anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022\\\" } } }\\n```\\n\\nNow you can run this command in the TUI:\\n\\n```\\n/test\\n```\\n\\n* * *\\n\\n### Markdown\\n\\nYou can also define commands using markdown files. Place them in:\\n\\n* Global: `~/.config/opencode/commands/`\\n* Per-project: `.opencode/commands/`\\n\\n~/.config/opencode/commands/test.md\\n\\n```\\n--- description : Run tests with coverage agent : build model : anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 --- Run the full test suite with coverage report and show any failures. Focus on the failing tests and suggest fixes.\\n```\\n\\nThe markdown file name becomes the command name. They help automate repetitive tasks and standardize workflows.\\n\\n## Installation\\n\\nCopy command files to your OpenCode command directory:\\n\\n```\\n#  Per-project (recommended) \\ncp git/ * .md .opencode/command/\\ncp code-quality/ * .md .opencode/command/\\n\\n #  Or global \\ncp git/ * .md ~ /.config/opencode/command/\\n```\\n\\n## Available Commands\\n\\n### 冒聼聯聛 `git/` \\\\- Git & Version Control\\n\\n|Command |Description |\\n| --- | --- |\\n|`commit.md` |Smart git commit with conventional prefixes |\\n|`breaking.md` |Breaking change release with CHANGELOG update |\\n|`pr-review.md` |Review PR changes and suggest improvements |\\n|`issues.md` |Search GitHub issues |\\n\\n### 冒聼聯聛 `code-quality/` \\\\- Code Quality\\n\\n|Command |Description |\\n| --- | --- |\\n|`rmslop.md` |Remove AI-generated code slop |\\n|`spellcheck.md` |Check spelling/grammar in markdown files |\\n|`fix-types.md` |Fix TypeScript type errors |\\n|`refactor.\\nmd` |Refactor code for maintainability |\\n|`optimize.md` |Performance optimization analysis |\\n\\n### 冒聼聯聛 `docs/` \\\\- Documentation\\n\\n|Command |Description |\\n| --- | --- |\\n|`docs.md` |Add documentation to code |\\n|`explain.md` |Explain code or concept in detail |\\n\\n### 冒聼聯聛 `development/` \\\\- Development\\n\\n|Command |Description |\\n| --- | --- |\\n|`test-gen.md` |Generate unit tests for a file |\\n|`debug.md` |Quick debug session helper |\\n\\n## Command Syntax\\n\\nCommands support:\\n\\n### Arguments\\n\\n```\\nCreate component: $ARGUMENTS\\n$1 - first arg, $2 - second arg\\n```\\n\\n### Shell Output\\n\\n```\\n! Place them in:\\n\\n* Global: `~/.\\nconfig/opencode/commands/`\\n* Per-project: `.opencode/commands/`\\n\\n~/.config/opencode/commands/test.md\\n\\n```\\n--- description : Run tests with coverage agent : build model : anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 --- Run the full test suite with coverage report and show any failures. Focus on the failing tests and suggest fixes.\\n```\\n\\nThe markdown file name becomes the command name. Include proper typing and basic structure.\\n```\\n\\nRun the command with arguments:\\n\\n```\\n/component Button\\n```\\n\\nAnd `$ARGUMENTS` will be replaced with `Button` .\\nYou can also access individual arguments using positional parameters:\\n\\n* `$1` \\\\- First argument\\n* `$2` \\\\- Second argument\\n* `$3` \\\\- Third argument\\n* And so on...\\n\\nFor example:\\n\\n.opencode/commands/create-file.md\\n\\n```\\n--- description : Create a new file with content --- Create a file named $1 in the directory $2 with the following content: $3\\n```\\n\\nRun the command:\\n\\n```\\n/create-file config.json src \\\"{ \\\\\\\"key\\\\\\\": \\\\\\\"value\\\\\\\" }\\\"\\n```\\n\\nThis replaces:\\n\\n* `$1` with `config.json`\\n* `$2` with `src`\\n* `$3` with `{ \\\"key\\\": \\\"value\\\" }`\\n\\n* * *\\n\\n### Shell output \\n\\nUse _! `command`_ to inject bash command output into your prompt.\\n\\nFor example, to create a custom command that analyzes test coverage:\\n\\n.opencode/commands/analyze-coverage.md\\n\\n```\\n--- description : Analyze test coverage --- Here are the current test results: ! It's designed to help you write code, understand codebases, fix bugs, and build features - all from the command line.\\n\\n**Key Characteristics:**\\n\\n* Terminal-native (TUI - Terminal User Interface)\\n* Works with any LLM provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.)\\n* Git-integrated for undo/redo functionality\\n* Highly customizable with agents, commands, and tools\\n* Can be used alongside your existing IDE\\n\\n* * *\\n\\n## OpenCode vs Cursor\\n\\n### Why Use OpenCode Over Cursor?\\n\\n|Feature |OpenCode |Cursor |\\n| --- | --- | --- |\\n|**Environment** |Terminal-based |IDE (VS Code fork) |\\n|**Integration** |Works with ANY editor |Must use Cursor IDE |\\n|**Workflow** |Terminal-centric developers |GUI-centric developers |\\n|**LLM Providers** |Any provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, local models) |Limited to supported providers |\\n|**Git Integration** |Built-in undo/redo with Git |File-level history |\\n|**Agents System** |Multiple specialized agents (Build, Plan, custom) |Single AI assistant |\\n|**Customization** |Highly configurable (agents, tools, commands) |Limited customization |\\n|**Sharing** |Share full conversation sessions |Share code snippets |\\n|**Cost** |Pay-per-use with your API keys |Subscription-based |\\n|**Remote Work** |SSH-friendly, terminal-based |Requires GUI, heavier |\\n\\n### When to Use OpenCode\\n\\n**Choose OpenCode if you:**\\n\\n* Prefer terminal-based workflows\\n* Want to keep your existing editor (Vim, Neovim, Emacs, VS Code, etc.)\\n* Need to work over SSH or on remote servers\\n* Want full control over which LLM provider you use\\n* Prefer pay-per-use pricing with your own API keys\\n* Need specialized agents for different tasks (planning vs building)\\n* Want Git-integrated undo/redo functionality\"\n This is a stateful request - they intend to learn the topic over multiple sessions.\n9: \n10: ## Teaching Workspace\n11: \n12: Treat the current directory as a teaching workspace. The state of their learning is captured in this directory in several files:\n13: \n14: - `MISSION.md`: A document capturing the _reason_ the user is interested in the topic. This should be used to ground all teaching. Use the format in [MISSION-FORMAT.md](./MISSION-FORMAT.md).\n15: - `./reference/*.html`: A directory of reference materials. These are the compressed learnings from the lessons - cheat sheets, reference algorithms, syntax, yoga poses, glossaries. They are the raw units of learning. They should be beautiful documents which print out well, and are designed for quick reference.\n16: - `RESOURCES.md`: A list of resources which can be explored to ground your teaching in contextual knowledge, or to acquire knowledge and wisdom. Use the format in [RESOURCES-FORMAT.md](./RESOURCES-FORMAT.md).\n17: - `./learning-records/*.md`: A directory of learning records, which capture what the user has learned. These are loosely equivalent to architectural decision records in software development - they capture non-obvious lessons and key insights that may need to be revised later, or drive future sessions. These should be used to calculate the zone of proximal development. They are titled `0001-\x3Cdash-case-name>.md`, where the number increments each time. Use the format in [LEARNING-RECORD-FORMAT.md](./LEARNING-RECORD-FORMAT.md).\n18: - `./lessons/*.html`: A directory of lessons. A **lesson** is a single, self-contained HTML output that teaches one tightly-scoped thing tied to the mission. This is the primary unit of teaching in this workspace.\n19: - `NOTES.md`: A scratchpad for you to jot down user preferences, or working notes.\n20: \n21: ## Philosophy\n22: \n23: To learn at a deep level, the user needs three things:\n24: \n25: - **Knowledge**, captured from high-quality, high-trust resources\n26: - **Skills**, acquired through highly-relevant interactive lessons devised by you, based on the knowledge\n27: - **Wisdom**, which comes from interacting with other learners and practitioners\n28: \n29: Before the `RESOURCES.md` is well-populated, your focus should be to find high-quality resources which will help the user acquire knowledge. Never trust your parametric knowledge.\n30: \n31: Some topics may require more skills than knowledge. Learning more about theoretical physics might be more knowledge-based. For yoga, more skills-based.\n32: \n33: ### Fluency vs Storage Strength\n34: \n35: You should be careful to split between two types of learning:\n36: \n37: - **Fluency strength**: in-the-moment retrieval of knowledge\n38: - **Storage strength**: long-term retention of knowledge\n39: \n40: Fluency can give the user an illusory sense of mastery, but storage strength is the real goal. Try to design lessons which build long-term retention by desirable difficulty:\n41: \n42: - Using retrieval practice (recall from memory)\n43: - Spacing (distributing practice over time)\n44: - Interleaving (mixing up different but related topics in practice - for skills practice only)\n45: \n46: ## Lessons\n47: \n48: A lesson is the main thing you produce 芒聙聰 the unit in which knowledge and skills reach the user. Each lesson is one self-contained HTML file, saved to `./lessons/` and titled `0001-\x3Cdash-case-name>.html` where the number increments each time.\n49: \n50: A lesson should be **beautiful** 芒聙聰 clean, readable typography and layout 芒聙聰 since the user will return to these later to review. Think Tufte.\n51: \n52: The lesson should be short, and completable very quickly. Learners' working memory is very small, and we need to stay within it. But each lesson should give the user a single tangible win that they can build on. It should be directly tied to the mission, and should be in the user's zone of proximal development.\n53: \n54: If possible, open the lesson file for the user by running a CLI command.\n55: \n56: Each lesson should link via HTML anchors to other lessons and reference documents.\n57: \n58: Each lesson should recommend a primary source for the user to read or watch. This should be the most high-quality, high-trust resource you found on the topic.\n59: \n60: Each lesson should contain a reminder to ask followup questions to the agent. The agent is their teacher, and can assist with anything that's unclear.\n61: \n62: ## The Mission\n63: \n64: Every lesson should be tied into the mission - the reason that the user is interested in learning about the topic.\n65: \n66: If the user is unclear about the mission, or the `MISSION.md` is not populated, your first job should be to question the user on why they want to learn this.\n67: \n68: Failing to understand the mission will mean knowledge acquisition is not grounded in real-world goals. Lessons will feel too abstract. You will have no way of judging what the user should do next.\n69: \n70: Missions may change as the user develops more skills and knowledge. This is normal - make sure to update the `MISSION.md` and add a learning record to capture the change. Confirm with the user before changing the mission.\n71: \n72: ## Zone Of Proximal Development\n73: \n74: Each lesson, the user should always feel as if they are being challenged 'just enough'.\n75: \n76: The user may specify an exact thing they want to learn. If they don't, figure out their zone of proximal development by:\n77: \n78: - Reading their `learning-records`\n79: - Figuring out the right thing to teach them based on their mission\n80: - Teach the most relevant thing that fits in their zone of proximal development\n81: \n82: ## Knowledge\n83: \n84: Lessons should be designed around a skill the user is going to learn. The knowledge in the lesson should be only what's required to acquire that skill. You teach the knowledge first, then get the user to practice the skills via an interactive feedback loop.\n85: \n86: Knowledge should first be gathered from trusted resources. Use `RESOURCES.md` to keep track of them. Lessons should be littered with citations - links to external resources to back up any claim made. This increases the trustworthiness of the lesson.\n87: \n88: For acquiring knowledge, difficulty is the enemy. It eats working memory you need for understanding.\n89: \n90: ## Skills\n91: \n92: If knowledge is all about acquisition, skills are about durability and flexibility. Make the knowledge stick.\n93: \n94: For skill acquisition, difficulty is the tool. Effortful retrieval is what builds storage strength. Skills should be taught through interactive lessons. There are several tools at your disposal:\n95: \n96: - Interactive lessons, using quizzes and light in-browser tasks\n97: - Lessons which guide the user through a list of real-world steps to take (for instance, yoga poses)\n98: \n99: Each of these should be based on a **feedback loop**, where the user receives feedback on their performance. This feedback loop should be as tight as possible, giving feedback immediately - and ideally automatically.\n100: \n101: For quizzes, each answer should be exactly the same number of words (and characters, if possible). Don't give the user any clues about the answer through formatting.\n102: \n103: ## Acquiring Wisdom\n104: \n105: Wisdom comes from true real-world interaction - testing your skills outside the learning environment.\n106: \n107: When the user asks a question that appears to require wisdom, your default posture should be to attempt to answer - but to ultimately delegate to a **community**.\n108: \n109: A community is a place (online or offline) where the user can test their skills in the real world. This might be a forum, a subreddit, a real-world class (budget permitting) or a local interest group.\n110: \n111: You should attempt to find high-reputation communities the user can join. If the user expresses a preference that they don't want to join a community, respect it.\n112: \n113: ## Reference Documents\n114: \n115: While creating lessons, you should also create reference documents. Lessons can reference these documents - they are useful for tracking raw units of knowledge useful across lessons.\n116: \n117: Lessons will rarely be revisited later - reference documents will be. They should be the compressed essence of the lesson, in a format designed for quick reference.\n118: \n119: Some learning topics lend themselves to reference:\n120: \n121: - Syntax and code snippets for programming\n122: - Algorithms and flowcharts for processes\n123: - Yoga poses and sequences for yoga\n124: - Exercises and routines for fitness\n125: - Glossaries for any topic with its own nomenclature\n126: \n127: Glossaries, in particular, are an essential reference. Once one is created, it should be adhered to in every lesson.\n128: \n129: ## `NOTES.md`\n130: \n131: The user will sometimes express preferences of how they want to be taught, or things you should keep in mind. This is the place to record those preferences, so you can refer back to them when designing lessons or working with the user.\n\n(End of file - total 131 lines)\n\x3C/content>",metadata:$R[1841]={preview:"---\nname: teach\ndescription: Teach the user a new skill or concept, within this workspace.\ndisable-model-invocation: true\nargument-hint: \"What would you like to learn about?\"\n---\n\nThe user has asked you to teach them something. It should be directly tied to the mission, and should be in the user's zone of proximal development.\n\nIf possible, open the lesson file for the user by running a CLI command.\n\nEach lesson should link via HTML anchors to other lessons and reference documents.\n\nEach lesson should recommend a primary source for the user to read or watch. This should be the most high-quality, high-trust resource you found on the topic.\n\nEach lesson should contain a reminder to ask followup questions to the agent. The agent is their teacher, and can assist with anything that's unclear.\n\n## The Mission\n\nEvery lesson should be tied into the mission - the reason that the user is interested in learning about the topic.\n\nIf the user is unclear about the mission, or the `MISSION.md` is not populated, your first job should be to question the user on why they want to learn this.\n\nFailing to understand the mission will mean knowledge acquisition is not grounded in real-world goals. Lessons will feel too abstract. You will have no way of judging what the user should do next.\n\nMissions may change as the user develops more skills and knowledge. This is normal - make sure to update the `MISSION.md` and add a learning record to capture the change. Confirm with the user before changing the mission.\n\n## Zone Of Proximal Development\n\nEach lesson, the user should always feel as if they are being challenged 'just enough'.\n\nThe user may specify an exact thing they want to learn. If they don't, figure out their zone of proximal development by:\n\n- Reading their `learning-records`\n- Figuring out the right thing to teach them based on their mission\n- Teach the most relevant thing that fits in their zone of proximal development\n\n## Knowledge\n\nLessons should be designed around a skill the user is going to learn. The knowledge in the lesson should be only what's required to acquire that skill. You teach the knowledge first, then get the user to practice the skills via an interactive feedback loop.\n\nKnowledge should first be gathered from trusted resources. Use `RESOURCES.md` to keep track of them. Lessons should be littered with citations - links to external resources to back up any claim made. This increases the trustworthiness of the lesson.\n\nFor acquiring knowledge, difficulty is the enemy. It eats working memory you need for understanding.\n\n## Skills\n\nIf knowledge is all about acquisition, skills are about durability and flexibility. Make the knowledge stick.\n\nFor skill acquisition, difficulty is the tool. Effortful retrieval is what builds storage strength. Skills should be taught through interactive lessons. There are several tools at your disposal:\n\n- Interactive lessons, using quizzes and light in-browser tasks\n- Lessons which guide the user through a list of real-world steps to take (for instance, yoga poses)\n\nEach of these should be based on a **feedback loop**, where the user receives feedback on their performance. This feedback loop should be as tight as possible, giving feedback immediately - and ideally automatically.\n\nFor quizzes, each answer should be exactly the same number of words (and characters, if possible). Don't give the user any clues about the answer through formatting.\n\n## Acquiring Wisdom\n\nWisdom comes from true real-world interaction - testing your skills outside the learning environment.\n\nWhen the user asks a question that appears to require wisdom, your default posture should be to attempt to answer - but to ultimately delegate to a **community**.\n\nA community is a place (online or offline) where the user can test their skills in the real world. This might be a forum, a subreddit, a real-world class (budget permitting) or a local interest group.\n\nYou should attempt to find high-reputation communities the user can join. If the user expresses a preference that they don't want to join a community, respect it.\n\n## Reference Documents\n\nWhile creating lessons, you should also create reference documents. Lessons can reference these documents - they are useful for tracking raw units of knowledge useful across lessons.\n\nLessons will rarely be revisited later - reference documents will be. They should be the compressed essence of the lesson, in a format designed for quick reference.\n\nSome learning topics lend themselves to reference:\n\n- Syntax and code snippets for programming\n- Algorithms and flowcharts for processes\n- Yoga poses and sequences for yoga\n- Exercises and routines for fitness\n- Glossaries for any topic with its own nomenclature\n\nGlossaries, in particular, are an essential reference. Once one is created, it should be adhered to in every lesson.\n\n## `NOTES.md`\n\nThe user will sometimes express preferences of how they want to be taught, or things you should keep in mind. This is the place to record those preferences, so you can refer back to them when designing lessons or working with the user.",lineStart:1,lineEnd:131,totalLines:131,truncated:!1}},title:"Users\\HUAWEI\\.agents\\skills\\teach\\SKILL.md",time:$R[1844]={start:1781600272083,end:1781600272095}},id:"prt_ecfa6be2c001LBSnWSl8foSZYH",sessionID:"ses_130a18cecffe7uOj3xLvOWolPq",messageID:"msg_ecfa6a59a002Abtn3p3BAII830"},$R[1845]={reason:"tool-calls",type:"step-finish",tokens:$R[1846]={total:52667,input:6458,output:62,reasoning:195,cache:$R[1847]={write:0,read:45952}},cost:0,id:"prt_ecfa6bf320010MJAU2MGHLRrr0",sessionID:"ses_130a18cecffe7uOj3xLvOWolPq",messageID:"msg_ecfa6a59a002Abtn3p3BAII830"}],msg_ecfa6bf3c002HV5f7SkTdMk6zS:$R[1848]=[$R[1849]={type:"step-start",id:"prt_ecfa6c631001EYK2h8QMtrIJY0",sessionID:"ses_130a18cecffe7uOj3xLvOWolPq",messageID:"msg_ecfa6bf3c002HV5f7SkTdMk6zS"},$R[1850]={type:"reasoning",text:"Now I have a good understanding of the teach skill. # MISSION.md Format\n2: \n3: `MISSION.md` lives at the workspace root. It captures the _reason_ the user is learning this topic. Every teaching decision 芒聙聰 what to teach next, which resources to surface, which exercises to design 芒聙聰 should trace back to this document.\n4: \n5: ## Template\n6: \n7: ```md\n8: # Mission: {Topic}\n9: \n10: ## Why\n11: {1-3 sentences. The concrete real-world goal the user is chasing. What changes in their life or work when they have this skill? Avoid abstract framings like \"to understand X\" 芒聙聰 push for the underlying outcome.}\n12: \n13: ## Success looks like\n14: - {A specific, observable thing the user will be able to do}\n15: - {Another specific thing}\n16: - {芒聙娄}\n17: \n18: ## Constraints\n19: - {Time, budget, prior commitments, learning preferences, anything that bounds the approach}\n20: \n21: ## Out of scope\n22: - {Adjacent topics the user explicitly does not want to chase right now 芒聙聰 protects the zone of proximal development}\n23: ```\n24: \n25: ## Rules\n26: \n27: - **One mission per workspace.** If the user wants to learn two unrelated things, that is two workspaces.\n28: - **Concrete over abstract.** \"Run a half marathon by October\" beats \"get fitter.\" \"Ship a Rust CLI to my team\" beats \"learn Rust.\"\n29: - **Push back on vagueness.** If the user cannot articulate why, interview them before writing anything. A bad mission is worse than no mission.\n30: - **Revise when reality shifts.** Missions change. When the user's goal moves, update this file 芒聙聰 don't leave a stale mission steering future sessions.\n31: - **Keep it short.** If `MISSION.md` runs past a screen, it has stopped being a compass and started being a plan.\n\n(End of file - total 31 lines)\n\x3C/content>",metadata:$R[1883]={preview:"# MISSION.md Format\n\n`MISSION.md` lives at the workspace root. They are used to calculate the zone of proximal development.\n6: \n7: ## Template\n8: \n9: ```md\n10: # {Short title of what was learned or established}\n11: \n12: {1-3 sentences: what was learned (or what prior knowledge was established), and why it matters for future sessions.}\n13: ```\n14: \n15: That is the whole format. A learning record can be a single paragraph. The value is recording _that_ this is now known and _why_ it changes what to teach next 芒聙聰 not in filling out sections.\n16: \n17: ## Optional sections\n18: \n19: Only include these when they add genuine value. Most records won't need them.\n20: \n21: - **Status** frontmatter (`active | superseded by LR-NNNN`) 芒聙聰 useful when an earlier understanding turns out to be wrong and is replaced.\n22: - **Evidence** 芒聙聰 how the user demonstrated the understanding (a question answered, an exercise completed, prior experience cited). Useful when the claim might be revisited.\n23: - **Implications** 芒聙聰 what this unlocks or rules out for future sessions. Worth recording when non-obvious.\n24: \n25: ## Numbering\n26: \n27: Scan `./learning-records/` for the highest existing number and increment by one.\n28: \n29: ## When to write a learning record\n30: \n31: Write one when any of these is true:\n32: \n33: 1. **The user demonstrated genuine understanding of something non-trivial** 芒聙聰 not just exposure, but evidence they can use the concept correctly. This sets a new floor for what to teach next.\n34: 2. **The user disclosed prior knowledge** 芒聙聰 \"I already know X.\" Record it so future sessions don't re-teach it. Also record the _depth_ claimed.\n35: 3. **A misconception was corrected** 芒聙聰 the user previously believed something wrong and now sees why. These are high-value: they predict future stumbling blocks for related topics.\n36: 4. **The mission shifted in response to learning** 芒聙聰 the user discovered they cared about something different than they thought. Cross-link to [[MISSION.md]] and update it.\n37: \n38: ### What does _not_ qualify\n39: \n40: - Material that was merely covered. Coverage is not learning. Wait for evidence.\n41: - Anything already captured tersely in [[GLOSSARY.md]] as a term definition. Don't duplicate.\n42: - Session-by-session activity logs. Learning records are not a journal 芒聙聰 they are decision-grade insights.\n43: \n44: ## Supersession\n45: \n46: When a later record contradicts an earlier one (the user's understanding deepened or corrected), mark the old record `Status: superseded by LR-NNNN` rather than deleting it. The history of how understanding evolved is itself useful signal.\n\n(End of file - total 46 lines)\n\x3C/content>",metadata:$R[1890]={preview:"# Learning Record Format\n\nLearning records live in `./learning-records/` and use sequential numbering: `0001-slug.md`, `0002-slug.md`, etc. Create the directory lazily 芒聙聰 only when the first record is written.\n\nThey are the teaching equivalent of ADRs: they capture non-obvious lessons, key insights, and stated prior knowledge that will steer future sessions. They are used to calculate the zone of proximal development.\n\n## Template\n\n```md\n# {Short title of what was learned or established}\n\n{1-3 sentences: what was learned (or what prior knowledge was established), and why it matters for future sessions.}\n```\n\nThat is the whole format. They are used to calculate the zone of proximal development.\n\n## Template\n\n```md\n# {Short title of what was learned or established}\n\n{1-3 sentences: what was learned (or what prior knowledge was established), and why it matters for future sessions.}\n```\n\nThat is the whole format. A learning record can be a single paragraph. The value is recording _that_ this is now known and _why_ it changes what to teach next 芒聙聰 not in filling out sections.\n\n## Optional sections\n\nOnly include these when they add genuine value. Most records won't need them.\n\n- **Status** frontmatter (`active | superseded by LR-NNNN`) 芒聙聰 useful when an earlier understanding turns out to be wrong and is replaced.\n- **Evidence** 芒聙聰 how the user demonstrated the understanding (a question answered, an exercise completed, prior experience cited). Useful when the claim might be revisited.\n- **Implications** 芒聙聰 what this unlocks or rules out for future sessions. Worth recording when non-obvious.\n\n## Numbering\n\nScan `./learning-records/` for the highest existing number and increment by one.\n\n## When to write a learning record\n\nWrite one when any of these is true:\n\n1. **The user demonstrated genuine understanding of something non-trivial** 芒聙聰 not just exposure, but evidence they can use the concept correctly. This sets a new floor for what to teach next.\n2. **The user disclosed prior knowledge** 芒聙聰 \"I already know X.\" Record it so future sessions don't re-teach it. Also record the _depth_ claimed.\n3. **A misconception was corrected** 芒聙聰 the user previously believed something wrong and now sees why. These are high-value: they predict future stumbling blocks for related topics.\n4. **The mission shifted in response to learning** 芒聙聰 the user discovered they cared about something different than they thought. Cross-link to [[MISSION.md]] and update it.\n\n### What does _not_ qualify\n\n- Material that was merely covered. Coverage is not learning. Wait for evidence.\n- Anything already captured tersely in [[GLOSSARY.md]] as a term definition. Don't duplicate.\n- Session-by-session activity logs. Learning records are not a journal 芒聙聰 they are decision-grade insights.\n\n## Supersession\n\nWhen a later record contradicts an earlier one (the user's understanding deepened or corrected), mark the old record `Status: superseded by LR-NNNN` rather than deleting it. Knowledge for explainers should be drawn from here, not from parametric guesses. Wisdom comes from the communities listed here.\n4: \n5: ## Structure\n6: \n7: ```md\n8: # {Topic} Resources\n9: \n10: ## Knowledge\n11: \n12: - [Book: _The Science and Practice of Strength Training_ 芒聙聰 Zatsiorsky & Kraemer](https://example.com)\n13:   Foundational text on programming and adaptation. Use for: anything to do with periodisation, recovery, intensity zones.\n14: - [Article: \"How Much Should I Train?\" 芒聙聰 Greg Nuckols (Stronger By Science)](https://example.com)\n15:   Evidence-based review of volume landmarks. Use for: weekly set targets per muscle group.\n16: \n17: ## Wisdom (Communities)\n18: \n19: - [r/weightroom](https://reddit.com/r/weightroom)\n20:   High-signal subreddit, moderated against bro-science. Use for: programme critique, plateau troubleshooting.\n21: - Local: Tuesday strength class at {gym name}\n22:   Use for: real-time coaching feedback on lifts.\n23: ```\n24: \n25: ## Rules\n26: \n27: - **High-trust only.** Prefer primary sources, recognised experts, peer-reviewed work, and communities with strong moderation. If a resource is marketing dressed as education, leave it out.\n28: - **Annotate every entry.** A bare link is useless in three months. Add one line: what it covers and when to reach for it.\n29: - **Group by Knowledge / Wisdom.** Mirrors the philosophy in [SKILL.md](./SKILL.md). It is fine for a resource to appear in only one group.\n30: - **Surface gaps explicitly.** If no good resource exists for an area the mission needs, write a `## Gaps` section listing what is missing. This drives future search.\n31: - **Prune ruthlessly.** A resource that turned out to be wrong, shallow, or off-mission should be removed, not buried. Better five sharp sources than thirty mediocre ones.\n32: - **Record community preferences.** If the user has opted out of joining communities, note it here so future sessions don't keep proposing them.\n\n(End of file - total 32 lines)\n\x3C/content>",metadata:$R[1897]={preview:"# RESOURCES.md Format\n\n`RESOURCES.md` is the curated set of trusted sources for this topic. Knowledge for explainers should be drawn from here, not from parametric guesses. Wisdom comes from the communities listed here.\n\n## Structure\n\n```md\n# {Topic} Resources\n\n## Knowledge\n\n- [Book: _The Science and Practice of Strength Training_ 芒聙聰 Zatsiorsky & Kraemer](https://example.com)\n  Foundational text on programming and adaptation. Use for: anything to do with periodisation, recovery, intensity zones.\n- [Article: \"How Much Should I Train?\" 芒聙聰 Greg Nuckols (Stronger By Science)](https://example.com)\n  Evidence-based review of volume landmarks. Use for: weekly set targets per muscle group.\n\n## Wisdom (Communities)\n\n- [r/weightroom](https://reddit.com/r/weightroom)\n  High-signal subreddit, moderated against bro-science. Use for: programme critique, plateau troubleshooting.\n- Local: Tuesday strength class at {gym name}\n  Use for: real-time coaching feedback on lifts.\n```\n\n## Rules\n\n- **High-trust only.** Prefer primary sources, recognised experts, peer-reviewed work, and communities with strong moderation. If a resource is marketing dressed as education, leave it out.\n- **Annotate every entry.** A bare link is useless in three months. Add one line: what it covers and when to reach for it.\n- **Group by Knowledge / Wisdom.** Mirrors the philosophy in [SKILL.md](./SKILL.md). It is fine for a resource to appear in only one group.\n- **Surface gaps explicitly.** If no good resource exists for an area the mission needs, write a `## Gaps` section listing what is missing. This drives future search.\n- **Prune ruthlessly.** A resource that turned out to be wrong, shallow, or off-mission should be removed, not buried. All explainers, exercises, and learning records should adhere to its terminology. Building it is itself part of learning: compressing a concept into a tight definition is evidence the user understands it.\n4: \n5: ## Structure\n6: \n7: ```md\n8: # {Topic} Glossary\n9: \n10: {One or two sentence description of the topic this glossary covers.}\n11: \n12: ## Terms\n13: \n14: **Hypertrophy**:\n15: Muscle growth driven by mechanical tension and metabolic stress over repeated training sessions.\n16: _Avoid_: Bulking, getting big\n17: \n18: **Progressive overload**:\n19: Systematically increasing the demand on a muscle over time 芒聙聰 via load, volume, or intensity.\n20: _Avoid_: Pushing harder, levelling up\n21: \n22: **RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion)**:\n23: A 1芒聙聯10 self-rating of how hard a set felt, where 10 is failure and 8 means two reps left in the tank.\n24: _Avoid_: Effort score, intensity rating\n25: ```\n26: \n27: ## Rules\n28: \n29: - **Add a term only when the user understands it.** The glossary is a record of compressed knowledge, not a dictionary the user reads to learn. If the user has just been introduced to a concept, wait until they can use it correctly before promoting it here.\n30: - **Be opinionated.** When several words exist for the same concept, pick the best one and list the rest as aliases to avoid. This is how language compresses.\n31: - **Keep definitions tight.** One or two sentences. Define what the term IS, not what it does or how to do it.\n32: - **Use the glossary's own terms inside definitions.** Once a term is in the glossary, prefer it everywhere 芒聙聰 including inside other definitions. This is what makes complex terms easier to grasp later.\n33: - **Group under subheadings** when natural clusters emerge (e.g. `## Anatomy`, `## Programming`). A flat list is fine when terms cohere.\n34: - **Flag ambiguities explicitly.** If a term is used loosely in the wider field, note the resolution: \"In this workspace, 'set' always means a working set 芒聙聰 warm-ups are tracked separately.\"\n35: - **Revise as understanding deepens.** A definition the user wrote in week one may be wrong by week six. Update in place; do not leave stale entries.\n\n(End of file - total 35 lines)\n\x3C/content>",metadata:$R[1904]={preview:"# GLOSSARY.md Format\n\n`GLOSSARY.md` is the canonical language for this teaching workspace. All explainers, exercises, and learning records should adhere to its terminology. Building it is itself part of learning: compressing a concept into a tight definition is evidence the user understands it.\n\n## Structure\n\n```md\n# {Topic} Glossary\n\n{One or two sentence description of the topic this glossary covers.}\n\n## Terms\n\n**Hypertrophy**:\nMuscle growth driven by mechanical tension and metabolic stress over repeated training sessions.\n_Avoid_: Bulking, getting big\n\n**Progressive overload**:\nSystematically increasing the demand on a muscle over time 芒聙聰 via load, volume, or intensity.\n_Avoid_: Pushing harder, levelling up",truncated:!1,loaded:$R[1905]=[],display:$R[1906]={type:"file",path:"C:\\Users\\HUAWEI\\.agents\\skills\\teach\\GLOSSARY-FORMAT.md",text:"# GLOSSARY.md Format\n\n`GLOSSARY.md` is the canonical language for this teaching workspace. All explainers, exercises, and learning records should adhere to its terminology. Building it is itself part of learning: compressing a concept into a tight definition is evidence the user understands it.\n\n## Structure\n\n```md\n# {Topic} Glossary\n\n{One or two sentence description of the topic this glossary covers.}\n\n## Terms\n\n**Hypertrophy**:\nMuscle growth driven by mechanical tension and metabolic stress over repeated training sessions.\n_Avoid_: Bulking, getting big\n\n**Progressive overload**:\nSystematically increasing the demand on a muscle over time 芒聙聰 via load, volume, or intensity.\n_Avoid_: Pushing harder, levelling up\n\n**RPE (Rate of Perceived Exertion)**:\nA 1芒聙聯10 self-rating of how hard a set felt, where 10 is failure and 8 means two reps left in the tank.\n_Avoid_: Effort score, intensity rating\n```\n\n## Rules\n\n- **Add a term only when the user understands it.** The glossary is a record of compressed knowledge, not a dictionary the user reads to learn. If the user has just been introduced to a concept, wait until they can use it correctly before promoting it here.\n- **Be opinionated.** When several words exist for the same concept, pick the best one and list the rest as aliases to avoid. This is how language compresses.\n- **Keep definitions tight.** One or two sentences. Define what the term IS, not what it does or how to do it.\n- **Use the glossary's own terms inside definitions.** Once a term is in the glossary, prefer it everywhere 芒聙聰 including inside other definitions. This is what makes complex terms easier to grasp later.\n- **Group under subheadings** when natural clusters emerge (e.g. `## Anatomy`, `## Programming`). A flat list is fine when terms cohere.\n- **Flag ambiguities explicitly.** If a term is used loosely in the wider field, note the resolution: \"In this workspace, 'set' always means a working set 芒聙聰 warm-ups are tracked separately.\"\n- **Revise as understanding deepens.** A definition the user wrote in week one may be wrong by week six. Let me provide a more thorough analysis.\n\nThe key insight I missed before is the DESIGN PHILOSOPHY behind each component. Let me think about what really makes this skill tick:\n\n1. **MISSION.md** - Not just \"why learn this,\" but \"what observable thing changes when you have this skill.\" It's a compass, and it explicitly says: \"A bad mission is worse than no mission.\" It also has an \"Out of scope\" section to protect the zone of proximal development.\n\n2. **Learning Records** - These are NOT session logs. They are \"decision-grade insights\" - only written when the user demonstrates genuine understanding, discloses prior knowledge, a misconception was corrected, or the mission shifted. This is the key differentiator from a simple journal. The \"supersession\" mechanism is elegant.\n\n3. **Resources** - Organized into \"Knowledge\" (trusted sources) and \"Wisdom (Communities)\". Each entry is annotated with WHEN to use it. Gaps are explicitly surfaced.\n\n4. **Glossary** - A living document. Terms are only added when the user UNDERSTANDS them, not when they're introduced. \"Building it is itself part of learning: compressing a concept into a tight definition is evidence the user understands it.\" The glossary uses its own terms inside definitions for compounding understanding.\n\n5. **Lessons** - Each one is a self-contained HTML, beautifully designed for re-reading. ONE small thing per lesson. Links to reference docs and other lessons.\n\n6. **Reference docs** - The compressed essence. Unlike lessons which aren't revisited, reference docs are designed for quick lookup.\n\nThe PHILOSOPHY layer I missed:\n- **Knowledge 芒聠聮 Skills 芒聠聮 Wisdom** (three different types of learning, each needs a different approach)\n- **Fluency vs Storage strength** (the real goal is storage strength)\n- **Desirable difficulty** (retrieval practice, spacing, interleaving)\n- **Zone of Proximal Development** (challenge just enough)\n- **MISSION drives everything** - every teaching decision traces back to the mission\n\nNow, how does this apply to the user's \"忙聲聶忙聢聭氓娄聜盲陆聲忙聢聬盲赂潞莽篓聥氓潞聫氓聭聵\" feature?\n\nThe teach skill isn't about content delivery - it's about creating a structured learning workspace. I spent my high school summers volunteering with art programs and painting murals in community centers.  \\n  \\nI have designed products, brands, and websites for the best of the best in [fortune 5 big tech](https://business.google.com/us/merchant-center/) and [billion dollar startups](https://web.archive.org/web/20240502124440/https://www.getdbt.com/) . Working at that caliber is fascinating, on one hand it feels like grad school, the smartest people in the world creating together. but on the other hand,,,, I am an artist with a digital soul!! I want to work on projects that push the limits of what code can do!!! And I love working with fellow artists, founders, and small businesses.\\nNowadays I create art with talented people who value human craft, people who are great at what they do and need someone that can extend that universe to the digital realm. I design websites for [award winning independent fillmmakers](https://www.ladykingsoftexas.com) , [james beard award winning restaurants](https://www.corinnesplace.com) , [experiential street team marketing for billboard 100 bands](https://www.thesyn.com/case-studies/panic-at-the-disco-2) , and [created logos](https://www.jeyausten.com/showcase/hair-by-love-alternative-salon-brand-logo-design) , [merch, and ecommerce sites](https://www.jeyausten.com/showcase/how-gay-thou-art-podcast-branding-website-merch-design) for [alternative brands](https://www.jeyausten.com/showcase/elixir-aesthetics-alternative-skincare-brand-hand-lettered-logo) . In a world of vibe coding, taste matters more than ever, and I bring a lifetime of design experience.\\nWhen I芒聙聶m not designing, I芒聙聶m selling my paintings in galleries and markets across the US, podcasting about technology, queerness, and culture, or doing zombie makeup to announce your fave superbowl opening punk band's new tour. I芒聙聶ve had friends text me months later 芒聙聹thank you for always sharing your art, it motivated me to start my own芒聙聺 and that made it all worth it. We don芒聙聶t make work in a silo 芒聙聯 invite your friends to body double and work on your art together. You don芒聙聶t have to do it alone. Happy creating \x3C3.\\n\\n##### **Thanks, so before we move on maybe you can share a bit more about yourself?**\\n\\nI am a designer, artist, podcast host, and just generally a person trying to make my corner of the world a better place.\\n\\nBased in Austin, Texas, I am a freelance designer for Big Tech. I work in Design Systems 芒聙聯 which is a fancy way of saying that I make sure when you see a button on an app or website that it looks the same every time, follows a specific set of rules, and is accessible so that everyone is able to read the text and interact with the button.\\nInstead of just designing apps, I design the style guide on steroids that app designers and developers pull from to make apps. I芒聙聶ve done so many types of design in my career 芒聙聯 created brands, websites, apps, and everything in between. I love that I芒聙聶ve never had to pick an industry, sure I work in tech now, but I芒聙聶ve also designed for high-end restaurants, theme parks, higher education, etc. My career is non-linear. I went to school for film and theater, and used to do carpentry, welding, pyrotechnics, makeup art and costume design before deciding I actually wanted a paycheck and to occasionally go to the doctor.\\n\\nWhen I芒聙聶m not designing, painting, sculpting, or attending art shows, I cohost a podcast called How Gay Thou Art with Clint Keller. Each episode is an hour long comedic well-researched deep dive into niche topics in Evangelical Christianity such as Christian theme parks, adult toys, furries, and textbooks.\\nWhile I am a non-binary artist with no connection to religion now, it took a ton of therapy as someone raised in cult-like faith healing churches in Waco, Texas, and I want to peel back the veil on that world to give context to why Evangelicals target the queer community.\\n\\n##### **There is so much advice out there about all the different skills and qualities folks need to develop in order to succeed in today芒聙聶s highly competitive environment and often it can feel overwhelming. So, if we had to break it down to just the three that matter most, which three skills or qualities would you focus on?**\\n\\nCreative mentorship and networking 芒聙聯 any chance I get I am at the next design conference, Creative Mornings, or burlesque figure drawing. I love it because I芒聙聶ve met so many creatives 芒聙聯 ceramicists, illustrators, sculptors, painters, filmmakers 芒聙聯 who problem-solve in ways I would have never imagined. Cryptid goblin, I'm a creative's creative.  \n  \nI grew up online, learning html from neopets. My first website was a neon linktree of my favorite sites, local files saved on a floppy disk so I could bring it back and forth between my mom's and dad's houses. I spent my high school summers volunteering with art programs and painting murals in community centers.  \n  \nI have designed products, brands, and websites for the best of the best in [fortune 5 big tech](https://business.google.com/us/merchant-center/) and [billion dollar startups](https://web.archive.org/web/20240502124440/https://www.getdbt.com/). Working at that caliber is fascinating, on one hand it feels like grad school, the smartest people in the world creating together. but on the other hand,,,, I am an artist with a digital soul!! I want to work on projects that push the limits of what code can do!!! And I脗聽love working with fellow artists, founders, and small businesses.  \n  \nNowadays I create art with talented people who value human craft, people who are great at what they do and need someone that can extend that universe to the digital realm. I design websites for [award winning independent filmmakers](https://www.ladykingsoftexas.com), [james beard award winning restaurants](https://www.corinnesplace.com),脗聽[experiential street team marketing for billboard 100 bands](https://www.thesyn.com/case-studies/panic-at-the-disco-2), and [created logos](https://www.jeyausten.com/showcase/hair-by-love-alternative-salon-brand-logo-design), [merch, and ecommerce sites](https://www.jeyausten.com/showcase/how-gay-thou-art-podcast-branding-website-merch-design) for [alternative brands](https://www.jeyausten.com/showcase/elixir-aesthetics-alternative-skincare-brand-hand-lettered-logo). In a world of vibe coding, taste matters more than ever, and I bring a lifetime of design experience.  \n  \nWhen I芒聙聶m not designing, I芒聙聶m selling my paintings in galleries and markets across the US, podcasting about technology, queerness, and culture, or doing zombie makeup to announce your fave superbowl opening punk band's new tour. Sound like someone you want to work with?  \n\n[dm me \x3C3](#Footer)\n\n## Press, press, press, press, press\n\n[\n\n### Claws x Code\n\nHosts Joi Jetson and Jey Austen take you on a design journey through the Claws x Code podcast. The problem it solves: LLMs default to generic, mediocre UI because they lack grounded knowledge of color psychology, typography pairings, component token architecture, and platform-specific idioms. This skill pack embeds that knowledge as first-class context.\\n\\n## Mental model\\n\\n* **Skill** 芒聙聰 A named capability folder (e.g. `ui-styling` , `design` , `brand` ) containing a `SKILL.\\nmd` (prompt instructions), `references/` (design reference docs), `data/` (CSV lookup tables), and `scripts/` (Python generators). routes to the others.\\n* **Data layer** 芒聙聰 Large CSV files ( `colors.csv` , `styles.csv` , `typography.csv` , `google-fonts.csv` , etc.) that the AI searches at generation time to ground choices in real options rather than hallucinating.\\n* **Stack file** 芒聙聰 Per-framework CSVs under `stacks/` (react, nextjs, vue, flutter, swiftui, threejs, etc.) that provide idiomatic patterns for each platform.\\n* **CIP (Creative Image Prompt)** 芒聙聰 A structured prompt format for logo, icon, and social image generation, assembled from `design/data/cip/` CSV lookups.\\n* **Design token** 芒聙聰 The `design-system` skill works with a `design-tokens-starter.json` and token architecture (primitive 芒聠聮 semantic 芒聠聮 component) enforced by validation scripts.\\n\\n## Install\\n\\n```\\nnpm install -g It generates cohesive design systems (50 styles, 21 palettes, 50 font pairings) and component libraries for buttons, modals, navbars, sidebars, cards, tables, forms and charts (20 chart types). It supports eight implementation stacks (React, Next.js, Vue, Svelte, SwiftUI, React Native, Flutter, Tailwind) and emits ready-to-use snippets (.html, .tsx, .vue, .svelte).\\nCore capabilities include planning, creating, implementing, reviewing, refactoring and optimizing UI/UX code; accessibility and responsive checks; animation, spacing, typography and color guidance; and performance and consistency enhancements. Use cases: landing pages, dashboards, admin panels, e-commerce, SaaS, portfolios, blogs and mobile apps. The Skill delivers faster prototyping, consistent visual systems, cross-stack portability and production-ready UI code with accessibility best practices.\\n\\n\\n\\n---\\n\\n## About LobeHub Skills Marketplace\\n\\nThis skill was found via the **LobeHub Skills Marketplace** 芒聙聰 the world's largest skills marketplace with over 100,000 skills. user is asking a philosophical question about why people stop updating their personal blogs/monthly updates after a year or two, even though knowledge accumulation should be compounding. Let me think about this carefully and give them a thoughtful answer.\n\nThis is a good question - it goes to the heart of what they're trying to build for themselves. Let me analyze it from a few angles without giving unsolicited advice.\n\nThe key insight: knowledge IS compounding, but **writing about it regularly** is a different behavior from knowledge accumulation itself. The two are often conflated.\n\nLet me think about why people fade out:\n\n1. **Writing for an audience vs writing for yourself** 芒聙聰 Most people start with excitement but eventually feel pressure to produce \"good content\" for readers. The audience expectation kills the joy.\n\n2. **The compounding effect is invisible** 芒聙聰 The benefit of writing weekly is that your thinking gets clearer over time. But this is an internal, gradual effect. There's no \"aha moment\" that rewards you. Meanwhile, the cost (sitting down to write) is immediate and visible.\n\n3. **Life gets in the way** 芒聙聰 Weekly/monthly cadence is hard to maintain when life changes (new job, relationship, health issues, etc.). Missing one week makes it easier to miss the next.\n\n4. **Perfectionism** 芒聙聰 Early posts are easy because nobody reads them. As you build an audience, the bar feels higher, and writing becomes stressful rather than joyful.\n\n5. **Purpose drift** 芒聙聰 The initial reason for writing (capture learning, express creativity) gets replaced by metrics (views, subscribers). When the metrics don't grow, motivation dies.\n\nThe user references Airing's article where he said \"猫驴聶盲潞聸盲赂聹猫楼驴氓聟露氓庐聻忙虏隆忙聹聣盲赂聙盲赂陋忙聵炉氓驴聟茅隆禄氓聛職莽職聞芒聙聰芒聙聰盲陆聠忙聢聭猫驴聵忙聵炉氓驴聧盲赂聧盲陆聫氓聨禄氓聛職盲潞聠茂录聦猫聙聦盲赂聰氓聛職莽職聞忙聴露氓聙聶氓录聜氓赂赂氓驴芦盲鹿聬.\" The key difference is: Airing writes because he WANTS to, not because he SHOULD.\n\nActually, I think the real answer is simpler and more profound.
